title: Language Generation in the Limit
speaker: Jon Kleinberg
abstract:
Although current large language models are complex, the most basic specifications of the underlying language generation problem itself are simple to state: given a finite set of training samples from an unknown language, produce valid new strings from the language that don't already appear in the training data. Here we ask what we can conclude about language generation using only this specification, without any further properties or distributional assumptions. In particular, we consider models in which an adversary enumerates the strings of an unknown target language that is known only to come from a possibly infinite list of candidate languages, and we show that it is possible to give certain non-trivial guarantees for language generation in this setting. The resulting guarantees contrast dramatically with negative results due to Gold and Angluin in a well-studied model of language learning where the goal is to identify an unknown language from samples; the difference between these results suggests that identifying a language is a fundamentally different problem than generating from it. 
The talk will cover joint work with Sendhil Mullainathan and with Fan Wei.

proof_stages.label: LLM abstraction to formal language generation
proof_stages.summary: The talk isolates an hourglass-level abstraction above neural-network implementation: observed strings over an alphabet come from an unknown language, and the desired functionality is to generate new valid strings.

proof_stages.label: Gold–Angluin identification-in-the-limit barrier
proof_stages.summary: Gold’s adversarial positive-data game and Angluin’s characterization show that exact language identification in the limit is impossible even for very simple language families, motivating a different objective.

proof_stages.label: Generation-in-the-limit game and main possibility theorem
proof_stages.summary: The objective is weakened from identifying the target language to eventually outputting unseen strings inside it, and the main theorem asserts that every countable family of infinite languages is generable in the limit.

proof_stages.label: Failure of closure and sequential-hypothesis strategies
proof_stages.summary: Two natural one-paragraph approaches fail: closure may contain no new guaranteed strings in infinite families, and scanning hypotheses by index can be trapped forever by an earlier strict superset of the true language.

proof_stages.label: Critical languages and oracle proof of generation
proof_stages.summary: A language is declared critical when it is consistent with the data and contained in every earlier consistent language; the true language eventually becomes critical, so choosing the rightmost critical language in a growing finite window eventually yields only strings from a subset of the truth.

proof_stages.label: Finite-prefix implementation without subset oracles
proof_stages.summary: The noncomputable subset test in criticality is replaced by finite-prefix t,m-criticality, giving an actual membership-query algorithm that keeps increasing prefixes while retaining eventual safety inside the true language.

proof_stages.label: Finite-family a priori bound via closure
proof_stages.summary: For a finite collection of candidate languages, a closure/intersection argument yields a finite sample bound after which generation is guaranteed, with follow-up notions such as finite closure dimension extending this perspective.

proof_stages.label: Breadth, density, mode collapse, and prompted generation
proof_stages.summary: Later refinements quantify the breadth of generated subsets by density, show an algorithm can return to the true language infinitely often while zero-density collapse remains unavoidable in some instances, and extend the framework to prompted generation and empirical expressiveness questions.

examples.name: All strings versus even-length strings
examples.note: This two-language family illustrates why identification may have no a priori success time while generation can safely stay inside a common subset.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the all-strings versus even-length-strings case, showing how identification can be forced wrong and how generation succeeds by outputting even-length strings.

passes.reason: The pair includes the trivial maximum language of all strings and is used to refute any fixed-time bound for identification.

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through arithmetic progressions by deriving outputs from observed points, e.g. using −3 and 3 to generate 9, 15, 21, and using them to illustrate Gold’s argument.
